mission.title: Install and Configure Elastic Cloud on Kubernetes (ECK)
mission.description: Elastic Cloud on Kubernetes (ECK) automates the deployment and management of Elastic Stack components on Kubernetes. It is ideal for users looking to run Elasticsearch, Kibana, and other Elastic products in a Kubernetes environment. Key trade-offs include the need for Kubernetes expertise and potential complexity in configuration.

steps.title: Add the ECK Helm repository
steps.description:
This step adds the Elastic Helm repository to your Helm configuration, allowing you to install ECK from it.

```bash

helm repo add elastic https://helm.elastic.co

helm repo update

```

> This command adds the official Elastic Helm repository.
> Updates the local Helm chart repository cache.

steps.title: Install the ECK operator
steps.description:
This step installs the ECK operator in the 'elastic-system' namespace using Helm.

```bash

kubectl create namespace elastic-system

helm install elastic-operator elastic/eck-operator --namespace elastic-system --version 2.14.0

```

> Creates a namespace for the ECK operator.
> Installs the ECK operator with a specific version.

steps.title: Verify the ECK operator installation
steps.description:
This step checks if the ECK operator is running correctly in the cluster.

```bash

kubectl get pods --namespace elastic-system

```

> Lists all pods in the elastic-system namespace to verify the operator is running.

steps.title: Create an Elasticsearch custom resource
steps.description:
This step creates an Elasticsearch cluster using a custom resource definition (CRD).

```bash

cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: elasticsearch.k8s.elastic.co/v1
kind: Elasticsearch
metadata:
  name: my-elasticsearch
  namespace: default
spec:
  version: 8.6.0
  nodeSets:
  - name: default
    count: 3
    config:
      node.store.allow_mmap: false
EOF

```

> Creates an Elasticsearch cluster named 'my-elasticsearch' with 3 nodes.
commands:
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: elasticsearch.k8s.elastic.co/v1
kind: Elasticsearch
metadata:
  name: my-elasticsearch
  namespace: default
spec:
  version: 8.6.0
  nodeSets:
  - name: default
    count: 3
    config:
      node.store.allow_mmap: false
EOF

steps.title: Verify the Elasticsearch cluster
steps.description:
This step checks if the Elasticsearch cluster is running and ready.

```bash

kubectl get elasticsearch my-elasticsearch

```

> Verifies the status of the Elasticsearch cluster.

uninstall.title: Uninstall the ECK operator
uninstall.description:
This step removes the ECK operator and all associated resources. Be aware that this may lead to data loss if Elasticsearch clusters are not deleted properly.

```bash

helm uninstall elastic-operator --namespace elastic-system

kubectl delete namespace elastic-system

```

> Uninstalls the ECK operator.
> Deletes the namespace and all resources within it.

upgrade.title: Upgrade the ECK operator
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you backup your Elasticsearch data. This step upgrades the ECK operator to a newer version.

```bash

kubectl drain <node-name> --ignore-daemonsets

helm upgrade elastic-operator elastic/eck-operator --namespace elastic-system --version 2.14.0

kubectl get pods --namespace elastic-system

```

> Drains the node to prepare for upgrade.
> Upgrades the ECK operator to the specified version.
> Verifies that the ECK operator pods are running after the upgrade.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Failed to install CRD'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may occur if the CRD is already installed or there are permission issues.

**Fix:**
Check existing CRDs with 'kubectl get crd' and ensure you have the necessary permissions.

troubleshooting.title: Elasticsearch cluster status is 'red'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates that some primary shards are unassigned, possibly due to insufficient resources.

**Fix:**
Check resource allocation and logs for errors, and ensure nodes are healthy.

troubleshooting.title: Kibana is not accessible
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** Kibana may not be properly configured or the service is not exposed.

**Fix:**
Ensure the Kibana service is running and check the service type (e.g., LoadBalancer).

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Failed to create Elasticsearch cluster'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may happen due to invalid configuration in the custom resource.

**Fix:**
Review the custom resource definition for errors and correct them.

versionNotes.changes: Added support for Elasticsearch 9.x, improved TLS certificate management.
versionNotes.deprecations: Deprecated the use of legacy configuration options in CRDs.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: If upgrading from 2.12 or 2.13, review the new CRD specifications for changes.

resolution.summary: You should see the Elasticsearch cluster status as 'green' when everything is working: 'NAME: my-elasticsearch, STATUS: green'.
codeSnippets:
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: elasticsearch.k8s.elastic.co/v1
kind: Elasticsearch
metadata:
  name: my-elasticsearch
  namespace: default
spec:
  version: 8.6.0
  nodeSets:
  - name: default
    count: 3
    config:
      node.store.allow_mmap: false
EOF

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running with the specified resources.
